questions.question: What is the transmission time of a 64-byte Ethernet frame at 10 Mbps?
options: 512 µs
questions.explanation: 64 bytes × 8 bits = 512 bits. 512 bits ÷ 10,000,000 bps = 51.2 µs. This value is also the 10 Mbps Ethernet slot time, chosen so the sender stays on the wire long enough for any collision to propagate back before the minimum frame finishes.

questions.question: Why does classic Ethernet define a minimum frame size of 64 bytes?
options: To reduce the number of frames a switch must buffer per second
To ensure the transmitter is still sending when a worst-case collision returns
To match the MTU of IPv4 datagrams exactly
To fit exactly one TDMA time slot on shared coaxial cable
questions.explanation: CSMA/CD is listen-while-talking, not just listen-before-talking. For collision detection to work, the sender must still be transmitting when the collision signal — which travels the full network diameter and back — arrives. The 64-byte minimum guarantees the sender's frame occupies at least one full slot time (512 bit-times) so no collision can arrive after the frame is complete.

questions.question: What is the bandwidth-distance product (bits in flight one-way) for a 500 m copper cable with signal speed 2×10⁸ m/s at 100 Mbps?
options: 25 bits
250 bits
2500 bits
25000 bits
questions.explanation: One-way propagation delay = 500 m ÷ (2×10⁸ m/s) = 2.5 µs. Bits in flight = 100×10⁶ bps × 2.5×10⁻⁶ s = 250 bits. The same cable holds only 25 bits at 10 Mbps, but at 1 Gbps it holds 2500 bits — showing why higher line rates make the same physical distance look much longer in bit-time terms.

questions.question: A 100 Mbps half-duplex segment reports late collisions. Which condition best explains this?
options: Too many small 64-byte frames are being sent simultaneously
The collision domain is oversized so the collision echo returns after the minimum frame has finished
Binary exponential backoff has reached its maximum retry limit of 16 attempts
The interframe gap is configured below the 9.6 µs minimum
questions.explanation: A late collision is detected after the first slot time (5.12 µs at 100 Mbps). This means the round-trip propagation delay of the collision domain exceeds the slot time, so the echo arrives after the sender has already finished the 64-byte minimum frame and believes it succeeded. Normal congestion causes early collisions; late collisions indicate an invalid collision domain, duplex mismatch, or faulty cabling.

questions.question: Why does switching from 10 Mbps to 100 Mbps on the same cable shrink the maximum allowed collision domain?
options: Higher speeds reduce the signal propagation velocity, making delays appear longer
The slot time is 10× shorter at 100 Mbps, so a collision must return 10× sooner to be detected before the minimum frame ends
Binary exponential backoff requires a smaller window at 100 Mbps
100 Mbps repeaters introduce more delay than 10 Mbps repeaters
questions.explanation: The slot time equals SLOT_TIME_BITS ÷ line_rate. At 100 Mbps that is 512 ÷ 100×10⁶ = 5.12 µs — exactly one-tenth of the 51.2 µs at 10 Mbps. The network diameter must shrink so the worst-case round-trip propagation fits inside 5.12 µs. Signal propagation speed is unchanged; only the bit-time is shorter, making the same physical distance consume more of the timing budget.

questions.question: Why do switched full-duplex Ethernet links not use CSMA/CD even though they still use the Ethernet frame format?
options: Full-duplex links run at speeds too high for collision detection hardware
Each port has dedicated transmit and receive paths with no shared medium, making collisions physically impossible
Full-duplex Ethernet uses larger frames that always fit inside a single slot time
The IEEE 802.3 standard removed CSMA/CD from the spec to simplify chip design
questions.explanation: In full-duplex mode each link is a point-to-point connection between a station and a switch port. Frames travel in one direction on the transmit wire and in the opposite direction on the receive wire; the paths are electrically isolated and never share bandwidth. Because no two senders ever compete for the same wire, contention is impossible and CSMA/CD — along with the slot time rule, minimum frame size constraint for collision detection, and binary exponential backoff — is simply never triggered.
